structured_choices.data_source: Historical card portfolio data spanning 5 years (60 months) from a single retail bank. Raw inputs: transaction-level logs (date, amount, merchant category code, authorization status), monthly account statements (balance, available credit, payment amount, payment status), credit bureau snapshots (pulled monthly or quarterly), and delinquency/charge-off outcome records from the bank's servicing system. All data sourced from production operational systems with no synthetic augmentation.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: charge-off status within 90 days of the observation window close. An account is labeled as 1 (charge-off) if it reaches 120+ days past due or is formally charged off within 90 days following the observation window end date. Accounts are labeled 0 (non-charge-off) if they remain current or are <120 days delinquent throughout the 90-day prediction horizon. Excluded from analysis: accounts closed by customer request, accounts refinanced to other products, accounts subject to hardship programs or proactive workouts, and accounts with policy-driven interventions during the prediction window (these are censored and not included in train or test sets, but their frequency is documented as a sensitivity metric).
structured_choices.split_method: Strictly temporal (out-of-time) split with no look-ahead. Training period: months 1–36 (3 years). Validation period: months 37–48 (12 months, used for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping). Test period: months 49–60 (final 12 months, touched only once after all model development). Justification: Credit risk is inherently temporal; macroeconomic regimes, customer behavior patterns, and policy changes evolve over time. A temporal split prevents the model from learning future patterns or leaking information about future account conditions into training. It also reflects the actual deployment scenario: the model will be trained on historical data and evaluated on truly prospective accounts. Random splits would violate causality and overstate generalization. Stratification is not applied by account characteristics because this would break temporal ordering; instead, we document the distribution of key confounds (account age, credit utilization, macro regime) across periods and check for drift.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training period (months 1–36) using only the training data available at the time. Validation and test sets are transformed using the fitted parameters from training, not refitted on their own data. This prevents information leakage and reflects deployment reality: the model is trained once, then applied to new accounts with the same preprocessing logic. Any missing values in validation or test periods that were not observed in training are handled via forward-fill within account history (if temporal) or the training-set-derived imputation mean (if cross-sectional), with a binary indicator flag added to signal such cases.
structured_choices.features: Transformer model input: Complete transaction sequences for each account within the observation window (e.g., prior 12 months before the prediction point), with each transaction represented as [date_offset, amount_normalized, merchant_category_embedding, transaction_type, authorization_status]. Merchant category embeddings are learned jointly during model training (not pre-trained external embeddings, to avoid external data leakage). Tabular baseline input: Monthly aggregated features computed from the same 12-month observation window: total transaction volume, mean/max/min transaction amounts, merchant category concentration (Herfindahl index), payment-to-balance ratio, delinquency indicator (current/30/60/90+ days past due), credit utilization ratio, account age in months, credit bureau risk score (most recent available), macroeconomic indicators (unemployment rate, credit card default rate index for the month). No feature may incorporate information from the 90-day prediction window or beyond it. Account-level demographic features (if available) are included in both models equally to control for this confound.
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based architecture: A sequence model consisting of a multi-head attention encoder (6–8 heads, 2–3 layers, hidden dimension 128–256) that processes transaction sequences, with positional encoding to capture temporal ordering. The transformer output (final hidden state or mean pooling over sequence) is concatenated with the tabular account-level features (account age, credit bureau score, utilization, recent delinquency status, macro features), then passed through 2–3 dense layers (128–256 units, ReLU activation) with dropout (0.3–0.5) to a sigmoid output layer for binary classification. Hyperparameters are tuned via random search or Bayesian optimization over the validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: Gradient Boosted Trees (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on the same monthly aggregated feature set as the transformer's tabular component. The baseline is given equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search (max depth, learning rate, number of rounds, regularization) is conducted over the same validation set and evaluated on the same metrics as the transformer. Both models use the same train/val/test splits, the same feature preprocessing (scaling/encoding fit on training only), and the same class weighting strategy to account for imbalance (described below). The baseline does not use raw transaction sequences; instead, it relies on summary statistics, which is the current industry practice and represents a fair comparison. Any performance gap attributable solely to sequence modeling can thus be isolated.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) computed on the test set. Justification: The business context involves severe class imbalance (charge-offs are typically 2–5% of the portfolio) and a threshold-dependent decision (only the top-risk accounts can be actioned). AUPRC is more informative than AUROC under imbalance because it focuses on performance in the positive class tail, which is operationally relevant. The bank cares most about discrimination at the top of the risk ranking: can we identify high-risk accounts with high precision and reasonable recall? AUPRC answers this directly. At a chosen operating point (e.g., top 5% by model score), we also report precision, recall, and F1 to show the specific trade-off being accepted.
secondary_metrics: AUROC on the test set — for comparison with industry benchmarks and to confirm that gains are not merely an artifact of threshold choice

Calibration: Brier score and calibration plots (predicted probability vs. observed frequency in deciles) — since downstream decisions (pricing, limit management) depend on absolute risk scores, not just ranking
Top-k precision at k=1%, 5%, 10% of the portfolio — directly aligned with intervention capacity
Sensitivity and specificity at the training-set-determined cost-optimal threshold — to evaluate performance balance
AUC separately for accounts stratified by tenure, credit utilization, and macro period — to detect whether the transformer advantage is real across subgroups or driven by confounds
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set (months 37–48). For each candidate hyperparameter configuration, the model is trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set using AUPRC as the selection metric. The top configuration (by validation AUPRC) is then retrained on the combined training + validation data (months 1–48) to maximize training signal before final evaluation on the test set. This is standard practice and does not inflate test performance estimates because the test set is held completely separate and not observed during tuning.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 49–60) is used exactly once, at the end of the experiment, to compute the final performance comparison. No model selection, threshold tuning, or metric optimization is performed on the test set. Final metrics (AUPRC, AUROC, calibration, top-k precision) are computed and reported with 95% confidence intervals via stratified bootstrap on the test set.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confounds and controls: (1) Recency effects and short-term volatility: Both models have access to account-level summaries (recent delinquency, recent payment behavior) computed over the same observation window, so recency is not a differentiator; the transformer's advantage, if any, comes from modeling sequence dependencies beyond simple aggregates. (2) Missingness patterns: Missingness is documented and flag indicators added to both models' feature sets. Imputation strategy is fit on training data only. (3) Macroeconomic regime: Macro indicators (unemployment, default rates) are included in both models' feature sets. Additionally, we stratify the test set by macro period (e.g., recession vs. expansion) and report separate metrics to verify that the transformer does not simply fit one regime better. (4) Account tenure and credit history length: Included as an explicit feature in both models. Test set performance is stratified by tenure to ensure the transformer does not exploit correlation between account age and risk. (5) Selection bias from policy-driven interventions: Accounts that are proactively managed, closed, or refinanced during the observation or prediction window are excluded from training and test sets; their exclusion rate and characteristics are reported separately. (6) Label noise: Charge-off status is operationally defined (120+ DPD or formal charge-off) and is authoritative; no subjective relabeling is performed. Sensitivity analysis: We rerun the comparison excluding the riskiest 1% of accounts (which may have different label quality due to manual review) and confirm findings hold.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Baseline for 90-Day Credit Card Charge-Off Prediction

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We will test whether a transformer-based sequence model, operating on full transaction histories and learned merchant embeddings, achieves superior discrimination of 90-day charge-off risk compared to a gradient-boosted baseline trained on monthly account aggregates. Both models will be evaluated on a strictly future out-of-time holdout set to ensure no leakage and to reflect real deployment conditions.

### 2. Data Strategy
We will use 5 years (60 months) of historical transaction logs, account statements, credit bureau snapshots, and outcome labels from the bank's production card portfolio. The raw data includes:
- Transaction-level records: date, amount, merchant category code, authorization outcome
- Monthly account summaries: balance, available credit, payment amount, delinquency status
- Credit bureau pulls (monthly or quarterly): risk scores and other signals
- Outcome: 90-day charge-off status determined from the bank's servicing system (120+ days past due or formal charge-off within 90 days of the observation window)

Accounts are excluded if they were closed by customer request, refinanced to other products, enrolled in hardship programs, or subject to policy-driven interventions (e.g., proactive limit reduction or workout) during the prediction window. We will document the exclusion count and characteristics to assess selection bias.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy
Data is divided strictly chronologically:
- **Training:** Months 1–36 (3 years)
- **Validation:** Months 37–48 (12 months; used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection)
- **Test:** Months 49–60 (12 months; held completely separate, touched only once at the end)

Justification for temporal split: Credit risk evolves with macroeconomic conditions, customer behavior, and policy changes. A temporal split prevents the model from learning future patterns and respects the causal ordering inherent in the problem. In deployment, the model will be trained on historical data and scored on truly prospective accounts; the temporal split mirrors this scenario. No random shuffling is applied, as it would create information leakage and invalidate the temporal assumptions.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Preprocessing workflow:** All scaling, encoding, and imputation transformers are fit on the training set only. Validation and test sets are transformed using the training-set-derived parameters. Missing values in validation or test are handled via forward-fill (for time-series within an account) or training-set imputation mean (for cross-sectional features), with a binary flag indicator added to signal imputation.

**Transformer model features:**
- Raw input: Complete transaction sequences from the 12-month observation window (prior to the prediction point)
- Each transaction: [days_offset_from_end, amount_normalized_to_account_scale, merchant_category, transaction_type, authorization_status]
- Merchant category embeddings: Learned jointly during training (dimension 16–32)
- Account-level features concatenated after sequence encoding: account age, credit bureau score, utilization ratio, current delinquency status, macro indicators (unemployment rate, industry default rate)

**Baseline (XGBoost/LightGBM) features:**
- Monthly aggregates from the same 12-month observation window: transaction count, mean/max/min amounts, merchant category Herfindahl index (concentration), payment-to-balance ratio, delinquency indicator, credit utilization, account age, credit bureau score, macro indicators
- No raw transaction sequences; instead, pre-computed summaries
- All preprocessing identical to transformer baseline features

Critical: No feature incorporates information from the 90-day prediction window or beyond. All features represent the account state at the observation window end date.

### 5. Model Architecture and Baseline

**Transformer model:**
Multi-head attention encoder (6–8 attention heads, 2–3 layers, 128–256 hidden dimensions) processes transaction sequences with positional encoding. The sequence representation (final hidden state or mean pooling) is concatenated with tabular account features, passed through 2–3 dense layers (128–256 units, ReLU, dropout 0.3–0.5), and projected to a sigmoid output for binary classification.

**Baseline (XGBoost or LightGBM):**
Gradient-boosted trees with tuned hyperparameters (max depth, learning rate, number of rounds, L2 regularization) trained on the monthly aggregates. Both models receive equal tuning effort: hyperparameter search (random search or Bayesian optimization) conducted on the validation set, using the same primary metric (AUPRC).

Why this baseline is fair: XGBoost on aggregated features is the industry standard for card risk modeling. Any performance gap can be attributed to the transformer's ability to extract sequence patterns, not to differences in tuning effort or preprocessing.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC)**
Justification: Charge-offs represent 2–5% of the portfolio (severe class imbalance). The bank can act on only a small fraction of accounts, so discrimination at the top of the risk list is paramount. AUPRC directly measures this: it prioritizes precision and recall in the positive (charge-off) tail and is insensitive to the imbalance ratio.

**Secondary metrics:**
- AUROC (for industry comparison and confirmation that gains are not threshold artifacts)
- Calibration: Brier score and decile-wise calibration plots (since downstream actions depend on absolute risk scores)
- Top-k precision at k = 1%, 5%, 10% (operational relevance: what fraction of flagged accounts are true positives?)
- AUC stratified by account tenure, credit utilization, and macro period (sensitivity: do gains hold across subgroups or are they confound-driven?)

### 7. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned via random search (or Bayesian optimization) over the validation set (months 37–48). For each configuration, the model is trained on the training set and scored on the validation set using AUPRC. The top configuration is selected, then the model is retrained on training + validation combined (months 1–48) to maximize training signal. The test set (months 49–60) is held completely separate and used only once, at the end, to compute final performance metrics with 95% confidence intervals via stratified bootstrap.

### 8. Confound Controls

We explicitly address known confounds:

1. **Recency effects:** Both models receive account-level summaries (recent delinquency, recent payment ratio) computed over the same window. The transformer's advantage, if real, comes from modeling sequence dynamics beyond aggregates.

2. **Macroeconomic regime shifts:** Both models include macro indicators. Test performance is stratified by period (recession vs. expansion) to verify no single regime is driving the gap.

3. **Account tenure:** Included as a feature in both models. Test stratification by tenure ensures the transformer does not overfit account age–risk correlation.

4. **Missingness patterns:** Missing-value indicators are added to both feature sets. Imputation is fit on training data only.

5. **Policy-driven selection bias:** Accounts with proactive interventions or refinancing are excluded from analysis, with exclusion rates and characteristics documented.

6. **Label quality:** Charge-off is operationally defined (120+ DPD or formal charge-off) with no subjective relabeling. Sensitivity: we rerun excluding the top-1% risk accounts (which may have elevated manual review) to confirm robustness.

### 9. Scope of Claims

This experiment will establish whether the transformer model has superior out-of-time discrimination and calibration on a future holdout. It does **not** establish causation (why the transformer is better) or generalization to other product lines or institutions. Results are specific to this bank's portfolio over this time period.
